canonical_summary:
Space compliance in the EU is not governed by a single regulator. It is a layered regime: the 1967 Outer Space Treaty fixes State responsibility under Article VI and launching State liability under Article VII, then thirteen Member States operate their own national licensing acts, the European Space Agency sets engineering standards through ECSS, and the European Union Agency for the Space Programme (EUSPA) operates Galileo, EGNOS, Copernicus user uptake, GOVSATCOM, IRIS² and the EU Space Surveillance and Tracking Front Desk under Regulation (EU) 2021/696. The gaps and overlaps are what the proposed EU Space Act, COM(2025) 335 of 25 June 2025, is designed to close.

For operators today, compliance means picking the right "appropriate State" for authorisation under Article VI, the right "launching State" or States for liability under Article VII, and the right Member State licence. France applies Loi 2008-518, the United Kingdom applies the Space Industry Act 2018, Luxembourg applies the Law of 20 July 2017, the Netherlands applies the Wet ruimtevaartactiviteiten, Belgium applies the Law of 17 September 2005, and Italy applies Law No. 89 of 13 June 2025. Germany still has no comprehensive act, so German operators rely on a foreign launching State licence plus bilateral responsibility allocation, the pattern Isar Aerospace used in Norway in 2025.

Once the EU Space Act applies on 1 January 2030, a single authorisation in the Member State of establishment, plus a launch certification in the Member State of launch, will be mutually recognised across the Union for safety, resilience and sustainability, with third country operators registered in the Union Register of Space Objects and represented by an EU legal representative.

faqs.question: Is there a single space regulator in the European Union?
faqs.answer: No. Space compliance in the EU is a layered regime. The Outer Space Treaty puts authorisation duty on each "appropriate State Party" under Article VI and launching State liability under Article VII. Thirteen EU Member States operate their own national space laws. The European Space Agency sets engineering standards through ECSS but is not a regulator. EUSPA, the European Union Agency for the Space Programme, operates Galileo, EGNOS, Copernicus user uptake, GOVSATCOM, IRIS² and the EU Space Surveillance and Tracking Front Desk under Regulation (EU) 2021/696, but it does not licence private launches. The proposed EU Space Act, COM(2025) 335 of 25 June 2025, is the first attempt to harmonise authorisation, safety, resilience and sustainability at Union level.

faqs.question: Which EU Member States have national space laws today?
faqs.answer: France applies Loi 2008-518 of 3 June 2008. The United Kingdom (outside the EU but a major hub) applies the Space Industry Act 2018. Luxembourg applies the Law of 20 July 2017 on the exploration and use of space resources. The Netherlands applies the Wet ruimtevaartactiviteiten. Belgium applies the Law of 17 September 2005, revised on 1 December 2013. Italy applies Law No. 89 of 13 June 2025, the Italian Space Economy Law. Austria, Denmark, Finland, Portugal, Greece and Spain also have acts. Germany has no comprehensive act, but the BMWK published Key Points for a German Space Act on 4 September 2024.

faqs.question: What does the French Loi relative aux opérations spatiales require?
faqs.answer: Loi 2008-518, in force since 10 December 2010, requires prior authorisation from the French government to launch or procure a launch, to control a space object in orbit, and to transfer control. A separate declaration regime applies to space data exploitation. Article 6 requires third party liability insurance or equivalent financial guarantee of 60 million euros for launch operations and 50 to 70 million euros for in-orbit operations, covering the State, its public bodies, ESA and its Member States. Above the ceiling, the French State indemnifies victims and has a right of recourse against the operator only for fault. The Technical Regulation governs debris mitigation and re-entry safety.

faqs.question: What does the UK Space Industry Act 2018 require?
faqs.answer: The Space Industry Act 2018 requires a licence for launch, procurement of launch, return, spaceport operation, range control and crewed stratospheric balloon operations above roughly 50 km. Operator licences, spaceport licences and range control licences are issued by the Civil Aviation Authority. Applicants must produce a Safety Case demonstrating risks reduced as low as reasonably practicable, an Assessment of Environmental Effects under Section 11, security and cyber strategies, and insurance covering operator liability. Section 3 makes unlicensed activity a criminal offence. Section 34 creates strict liability for injury or damage to third parties from spaceflight activities, with mandatory insurance set in licence conditions.

faqs.question: How does the Luxembourg Law of 20 July 2017 differ from other EU space laws?
faqs.answer: Luxembourg's Law of 20 July 2017 is the EU's first space resources statute. Article 1 declares that space resources are capable of being owned. No one may explore or use space resources without a written mission authorisation from the ministers in charge of the economy and space activities. Applicants must be a Luxembourg société anonyme, société en commandite par actions, société à responsabilité limitée, or a Societas Europaea with registered office in Luxembourg, with at least two managers showing senior space industry experience. Acting without authorisation under Article 2 carries 8 days to 5 years imprisonment and fines from 5,000 to 1,250,000 euros.

faqs.question: What is the role of the European Space Agency in compliance?
faqs.answer: The European Space Agency was created by the ESA Convention signed in Paris on 30 May 1975, which entered into force on 30 October 1980. ESA has 23 Member States and is governed by a Council on which each Member State holds one vote. Director General Josef Aschbacher took office on 1 March 2021. ESA is not a regulator. Its compliance role is to set engineering and quality standards through the European Cooperation for Space Standardization (ECSS), founded in 1993 and formally adopted by ESA on 23 June 1994. ECSS publishes M, Q, E, U, S and I branch standards used across European industry.

faqs.question: What does EUSPA do under Regulation (EU) 2021/696?
faqs.answer: EUSPA, the European Union Agency for the Space Programme, was launched on 12 May 2021 under Regulation (EU) 2021/696 as successor to the European GNSS Agency. It is headquartered in Prague. Executive Director Rodrigo da Costa took office on 16 October 2020. EUSPA operates Galileo, EGNOS, GOVSATCOM and the governmental user side of IRIS². It runs user uptake for Copernicus, security accreditation across all components through its independent Security Accreditation Board, and since 1 July 2023 has operated the EU Space Surveillance and Tracking Front Desk at the Galileo Security Monitoring Centre in Madrid for the 15 Member State EU SST Partnership.

faqs.question: Which State licenses a launch when the operator and the launch site are in different countries?
faqs.answer: The 1967 Outer Space Treaty puts the authorisation duty on the "appropriate State Party" under Article VI and liability on every "launching State" under Article VII, which the 1972 Liability Convention defines as a State that launches, procures the launching, or from whose territory or facility the launch takes place. In practice the operator applies for a launch licence in the territorial launching State. Isar Aerospace, headquartered near Munich, took its Launch Operator Permit from the Norwegian Civil Aviation Authority for the 2025 Spectrum flight from Andøya. Germany remained an appropriate State and covered residual obligations through bilateral arrangements.

faqs.question: What is the EU Space Act and when does it apply?
faqs.answer: The EU Space Act is the Proposal for a Regulation on the safety, resilience and sustainability of space activities in the Union, COM(2025) 335 final, procedure 2025/0335(COD). The Commission adopted it on 25 June 2025 on the legal basis of Article 114 TFEU. It harmonises three pillars: safety (trackability, collision avoidance, debris mitigation, manoeuvrability above roughly 400 km), resilience (a tailored cyber regime aligned with NIS 2 and a Union Space Resilience Network), and sustainability (a common Environmental Footprint methodology and lifecycle Environmental Footprint Declaration). Entry into force is the twentieth day after Official Journal publication, with date of application set to 1 January 2030.

faqs.question: Does the EU Space Act apply to non-EU operators?
faqs.answer: Yes. Articles 14 to 23 and 105 to 108 of COM(2025) 335 extend the Regulation to third country operators providing services in the EU. Non-EU operators must register their space objects in the Union Register of Space Objects, designate an EU legal representative and obtain an e-certificate. The Commission can recognise comparable jurisdictions through an equivalence mechanism, similar in spirit to the GDPR adequacy model. Defence and exclusively national security objects are carved out. The Council Presidency compromise of 30 March 2026, document ST 7806/2026, hardens mutual recognition: the Member State of establishment authorises the operator and the Member State of launch certifies the launch.

faqs.question: What insurance and liability caps apply across EU national space laws?
faqs.answer: Insurance ceilings vary. France caps third party liability insurance at 60 million euros for launch and 50 to 70 million euros for in-orbit operations under Article 6 of Loi 2008-518. The BMWK Key Points propose a 50 million euro liability insurance or bank guarantee cap for Germany. Italy's Law No. 89 of 13 June 2025 caps mandatory insurance at 100 million euros per incident. The UK Space Industry Act 2018 sets insurance levels in licence conditions and creates strict liability under Section 34. Belgium caps the State's right of recourse at 10 percent of the operator's annual turnover, uncapped where the operator acted without authorisation or in breach of conditions.

faqs.question: How should a foreign operator approach EU space compliance today?
faqs.answer: First, identify the "appropriate State" under OST Article VI: where is the operator established, and does that State have a licence regime. Second, identify every "launching State" under OST Article VII and the 1972 Liability Convention: the State that launches, procures the launch, or hosts the launch site. Third, apply in the territorial launching State for the operational licence, France, the United Kingdom, Italy, the Netherlands, Belgium or Luxembourg as relevant. Fourth, secure a bilateral responsibility allocation agreement with the home State. Fifth, design compliance to the ECSS standards ESA contracts cascade, and prepare for EU Space Act registration, EU representation and equivalence from 2030.

h1: Space compliance in the EU: national laws, ESA, EUSPA and the EU Space Act
meta_description: Space compliance in the EU explained: France LOS, UK SIA 2018, Luxembourg 2017, Italy Law 89/2025, ESA and ECSS, EUSPA programmes, and the 2025 EU Space Act.
meta_title: Space compliance in the EU: the complete 2026 guide

sections.heading: There is no single EU space regulator
paragraphs: Space compliance in the European Union is not run from one desk in Brussels. It is a layered regime that combines international treaty law, national licensing acts in thirteen Member States, the engineering standards system maintained by the European Space Agency, the operational mandate of the European Union Agency for the Space Programme, and the proposed EU Space Act of 25 June 2025.

The treaty layer is fixed. Article VI of the 1967 Outer Space Treaty obliges every State Party to authorise and continuously supervise the activities of its non-governmental entities in outer space, as the "appropriate State Party." Article VII makes every "launching State" internationally liable for damage caused by its space objects. The 1972 Liability Convention defines the launching State four ways: a State that launches, a State that procures the launching, a State from whose territory the launch takes place, and a State from whose facility the launch takes place. A single mission can therefore have three or four launching States at once.

The Union layer is younger and unfinished. Regulation (EU) 2021/696 created EUSPA and the EU Space Programme but did not harmonise authorisation, safety, resilience or sustainability for private operators. That is the gap COM(2025) 335, the proposed EU Space Act, is designed to close. Until it applies in 2030, operators navigate national laws plus the Outer Space Treaty plus ESA's standards.
bullets: Authorisation: national licence regimes under OST Article VI
Liability: the launching State under OST Article VII and the 1972 Liability Convention
Standards: ECSS, adopted by ESA on 23 June 1994 to replace the earlier PSS system
Operations: EUSPA for Galileo, EGNOS, Copernicus uptake, GOVSATCOM, IRIS² and EU SST
Harmonisation: the EU Space Act, COM(2025) 335 of 25 June 2025

sections.heading: The major national space laws across EU Member States
paragraphs: Thirteen EU Member States operate national space laws. The six most consequential for cross-border operators are France, the United Kingdom (outside the EU but a major launch jurisdiction), Luxembourg, the Netherlands, Belgium and Italy. Germany matters precisely because it has no comprehensive act yet.

subsections.heading: France: Loi 2008-518 of 3 June 2008 (LOS)
paragraphs: The French Space Operations Act has been in force since 10 December 2010. It requires prior authorisation to launch or procure a launch, to control a space object in orbit or on return, and to transfer control of a space object. A separate declaration regime governs the exploitation of space-origin data. Article 6 mandates third party liability insurance or equivalent financial guarantee of 60 million euros for launch operations and 50 to 70 million euros for in-orbit operations. Coverage must extend to the French State, its public bodies, ESA and its Member States, the operator, and persons participating in production or operation. Above the ceiling, the French State indemnifies victims and retains a right of recourse against the operator only for fault. The Réglementation Technique governs debris mitigation, end of life disposal and re-entry safety.

subsections.heading: United Kingdom: Space Industry Act 2018
paragraphs: The Space Industry Act 2018 covers launch, procurement of launch, operation or return of vehicles capable of operating above the stratosphere (around 50 km), spaceport operation, range control and crewed stratospheric balloon operations. Section 3 makes unlicensed spaceflight activity or spaceport operation a criminal offence. The Civil Aviation Authority issues operator, spaceport and range control licences. Applicants must produce a Safety Case demonstrating risks reduced as low as reasonably practicable (ALARP), an Assessment of Environmental Effects under Section 11, security and cyber strategies, and insurance covering operator liability. Section 34 creates strict liability for injury or damage to third parties from spaceflight activities, with mandatory insurance set in licence conditions.

subsections.heading: Luxembourg: Law of 20 July 2017 on space resources
paragraphs: Luxembourg's Law of 20 July 2017 was the EU's first space resources statute. Article 1 establishes that space resources are capable of being owned. No person may explore or use space resources without a written mission authorisation from the ministers in charge of the economy and space activities. Applicants must be a Luxembourg société anonyme, société en commandite par actions, société à responsabilité limitée, or a Societas Europaea with registered office in Luxembourg, with at least two managers showing senior-level experience in the space industry. Acting without authorisation under Article 2 carries 8 days to 5 years imprisonment and fines from 5,000 to 1,250,000 euros.

subsections.heading: Germany: no comprehensive act yet
paragraphs: Germany still has no general space act. Operations are licensed under sectoral instruments: the Satellitendatensicherheitsgesetz for high resolution Earth observation, the Raumfahrtaufgabenübertragungsgesetz allocating duties to DLR, and frequency licensing through the Bundesnetzagentur. On 4 September 2024 the Federal Ministry for Economic Affairs and Climate Action (BMWK) published Key Points for a German Space Act. The proposed Act would apply to German nationals and entities headquartered in Germany, install an authorisation regime, create a no-fault state right of recourse against operators, require liability insurance or bank guarantee capped at 50 million euros, mandate debris mitigation technical standards, and grant priority access by defence and state authorities to private space services.

subsections.heading: Italy: Law No. 89 of 13 June 2025
paragraphs: Italy's Space Economy Law is the youngest comprehensive framework in the Union. Published in the Gazzetta Ufficiale on 24 June 2025 and in force the following day, Law 89/2025 requires both Italian operators and foreign operators conducting space activities from Italian territory to obtain authorisation from the Ministry of Enterprises and Made in Italy (MIMIT). The Italian Space Agency (ASI) is the regulatory authority and operator of the national registry. Authorisations verify objective requirements (safety, resilience, environmental sustainability, debris mitigation, light and radio-electrical emissions impact assessments) and subjective requirements (operator conduct, technical capability, financial soundness, insurance). Mandatory insurance is capped at 100 million euros per incident.

subsections.heading: Netherlands: Wet ruimtevaartactiviteiten
paragraphs: The Wet ruimtevaartactiviteiten covers launching, flight operation and guidance of unguided and guided space objects from the Netherlands or from Dutch ships and aircraft, plus ground operation of satellites under Dutch jurisdiction (extended by ministerial regulation to operators of unguided satellites). Licence applications must be filed at least 6 months in advance with the Rijksinspectie Digitale Infrastructuur (RDI). Independent audits review financing, insurance, technical safety and operator competence. Licence conditions include debris mitigation, safe end of life disposal and third party liability insurance. The State has a right of recourse against the operator up to the limit fixed in the licence.

subsections.heading: Belgium: Law of 17 September 2005, revised 1 December 2013
paragraphs: The Belgian Act subjects launch, flight operation or guidance of a space object by Belgian-jurisdiction operators, or from Belgian territory or facilities, to prior authorisation from the minister in charge of space, with BELSPO managing the dossier. The 2013 revision clarified that for non-manoeuvrable in-orbit objects the operator is the party that contracted for launch under specified orbital parameters. A Royal Decree of 19 March 2008 sets implementing rules including safety, debris mitigation, environmental impact and registration. The State's right of recourse against the operator is capped at 10 percent of the operator's annual turnover or budget, uncapped where the operator acted without authorisation or in breach of conditions. SMEs benefit from reduced application costs of 1,000 euros.

sections.heading: ESA and ECSS: the standards layer
paragraphs: The European Space Agency is the source of European space engineering standards, not a regulator. The ESA Convention was opened for signature in Paris on 30 May 1975 by nine founding Member States: Belgium, Denmark, France, Germany, Italy, the Netherlands, Spain, Sweden and the United Kingdom. The Convention entered into force on 30 October 1980. Article II of the Convention sets ESA's purpose: "to provide for and to promote, for exclusively peaceful purposes, cooperation among European States in space research and technology and their space applications."

ESA today has 23 Member States and four Associate Members (Cyprus, Slovakia, Latvia, Lithuania). Canada participates through a Cooperation Agreement. The Council, where each Member State holds one vote, governs ESA. Director General Josef Aschbacher took office on 1 March 2021 and runs the Directorates from Paris, with major establishments at ESTEC (Noordwijk), ESOC (Darmstadt), ESRIN (Frascati), EAC (Cologne) and ECSAT (Harwell). Activities split into mandatory programmes (General Budget and Scientific Programme, funded by GNP-based scale) and optional programmes, with the geographical return principle derived from Article VII of the Convention and Annex V tying contracts to contributions.

Standards are coordinated through the European Cooperation for Space Standardization (ECSS), established in autumn 1993 on a Eurospace initiative and formally adopted by ESA on 23 June 1994 (resolution ESA/C/CXIII/Res). ECSS members include ESA, Eurospace, the Canadian Space Agency and other national agencies. The ECSS Secretariat is hosted at ESA-ESTEC. Standards are organised into branches: M for Space Project Management, Q for Space Product Assurance, E for Space Engineering, U for Space Sustainability (debris mitigation and planetary protection), S for general system documents, and the newer I branch for integrated systems. Documents come in three types: Standards (ST), Handbooks (HB) and Technical Memoranda (TM), supported by the EARM applicability matrix.

sections.heading: EUSPA: the operational EU agency under Regulation (EU) 2021/696
paragraphs: The European Union Agency for the Space Programme was officially launched on 12 May 2021 under Regulation (EU) 2021/696, the EU Space Programme Regulation. EUSPA is the successor to the European GNSS Agency (GSA), expanded to deliver the full EU Space Programme. It is headquartered in Prague, where the agency has been based since 2012, and operates across five sites including the Galileo Security Monitoring Centre (GSMC) in Madrid plus operations at Saint-Germain-en-Laye, Toulouse and Fucino. Executive Director Rodrigo da Costa took office on 16 October 2020. The agency runs roughly 270 staff and manages a programme envelope of about 9.5 billion euros across the 2021 to 2027 Multiannual Financial Framework.

subsections.heading: Galileo and EGNOS
paragraphs: EUSPA operates Galileo, Europe's global satellite navigation system, providing positioning, navigation and timing services, with service provision run through the GSMC in Madrid. EUSPA also owns service provision and market development for EGNOS, the European Geostationary Navigation Overlay Service, which augments GPS and Galileo for civil aviation, maritime and precision agriculture users.

subsections.heading: Copernicus
paragraphs: The Copernicus Earth observation programme is delivered by entrusted entities (ESA, EUMETSAT, ECMWF, Mercator Ocean and others), but Regulation (EU) 2021/696 gave EUSPA responsibility for user uptake, market development and security functions for Copernicus data and services.

subsections.heading: GOVSATCOM and IRIS²
paragraphs: EUSPA coordinates user-related aspects of Governmental Satellite Communications, pooling secure satellite capacity for EU and Member State authorities such as border guards and crisis management bodies. The GOVSATCOM Hub is the operational interface. For IRIS², the Infrastructure for Resilience, Interconnectivity and Security by Satellite, EUSPA handles security accreditation, operational management of the governmental infrastructure and provision of governmental services through the GOVSATCOM hub, while ESA leads technology development.

subsections.heading: EU Space Surveillance and Tracking (SST)
paragraphs: Since 1 July 2023 EUSPA has operated the EU SST Front Desk, taking over from the EU Satellite Centre. The Front Desk sits at the GSMC in Madrid and is the single interface between the EU SST Partnership (15 Member States: Austria, Czechia, Denmark, Finland, France, Germany, Greece, Italy, Latvia, the Netherlands, Poland, Portugal, Romania, Spain and Sweden) and users. It maintains the SST Portal and delivers three core services: collision avoidance, re-entry analysis and fragmentation analysis.

sections.heading: Cross-border launching State arrangements
paragraphs: The treaty obligations to authorise, supervise and indemnify do not align with company headquarters. They follow the State, and they can attach to several States at once. The 1972 Liability Convention defines a launching State four ways under Article I: the State that launches, the State that procures the launching, the State from whose territory the launch takes place, and the State from whose facility the launch takes place. ESA's Clean Space team summarises this as the "four-pronged definition."

For a company in a Member State without a national licensing law, the operational path is: identify which State is the "appropriate State" under OST Article VI and whether it has a licence regime; identify every candidate launching State under OST Article VII for liability and indemnity; then apply for the licence in the launching State. UNGA Resolution 68/74 (2013) recommends that States authorise launch, operation of launch sites and control of space objects in orbit, and that they define the scope of activities they will licence including activities by nationals abroad.

The live example is Isar Aerospace. Although headquartered near Munich, the company received its Launch Operator Permit from the Norwegian Civil Aviation Authority for the 2025 Spectrum flight from Andøya. Norway, as territorial launching State, took the front-line Article VI authorisation and Article VII liability exposure. Germany remained an appropriate State and covered its obligations through bilateral arrangements and general administrative law.

These bilateral arrangements are increasingly formalised. Luxembourg's 2020 Space Activities Law requires a written launching-State or responsibility-allocation agreement with the State that authorises or bears responsibility for the operator, guaranteeing Luxembourg is held harmless under the Liability Convention. France's Loi 2008-518 provides a state guarantee above a defined ceiling, reflected in Arianespace's contract architecture. ESA acts as a coordinator and convenor, not as a licensing authority.

sections.heading: The proposed EU Space Act: COM(2025) 335 of 25 June 2025
paragraphs: The EU Space Act is formally the Proposal for a Regulation of the European Parliament and of the Council on the safety, resilience and sustainability of space activities in the Union, document COM(2025) 335 final, procedure 2025/0335(COD). The Commission adopted it on 25 June 2025. The legal basis is Article 114 TFEU, which frames space governance as a single-market harmonisation file rather than a CSDP instrument. The Commission's stated problem is fragmentation: 13 Member States operate diverging national regimes, raising compliance cost for startups and SMEs and leaving gaps on debris mitigation and cybersecurity.

The proposal has three pillars. Safety (Title IV, Chapter I) introduces EU-wide rules on trackability, collision avoidance, debris mitigation plans, flight safety systems and manoeuvrability above roughly 400 km, with Article 73 covering constellations and Articles 101 to 103 covering in-space operations. Resilience (Chapter II) imports NIS 2 logic into space, with lifecycle cyber risk management, detection and reporting obligations and a new Union Space Resilience Network (EUSRN). Sustainability (Chapter III) creates a common Environmental Footprint methodology and a lifecycle Environmental Footprint Declaration feeding a Union database.

Articles 6 to 10 set Union-operator authorisation, with Article 49 introducing a single authorisation for whole constellations. Articles 11 to 13 cover Union-owned assets authorised by the Commission. Articles 14 to 23 and 105 to 108 extend the Regulation to third country operators providing services in the EU, requiring registration in the Union Register of Space Objects (URSO), designation of an EU legal representative and an e-certificate, with an equivalence mechanism for comparable jurisdictions. Defence and exclusively national security objects are carved out.

EUSPA gets a coordinating role under Articles 40 to 57, supported by a Compliance Board and a Board of Appeal. In Parliament, ITRE is the lead committee with rapporteur Elena Donazzan (ECR, Italy); the draft report was published 3 March 2026, with opinions from ENVI and IMCO. The Council Presidency compromise of 30 March 2026 (ST 7806/2026) places primary authorisation in the Member State of establishment and launch certification in the Member State of launch, with mutual recognition across the Union. The Regulation enters into force on the twentieth day after Official Journal publication, with date of application 1 January 2030.

sections.heading: A practical strategy for foreign operators
paragraphs: Foreign and EU-based operators face the same five-step compliance sequence today, and a slightly different one after 1 January 2030.
bullets: Identify the "appropriate State Party" under OST Article VI: where the operator is established and whether that State has a licence regime

Identify every "launching State" under OST Article VII and the 1972 Liability Convention: the State that launches, the State that procures the launching, the State from whose territory the launch takes place, and the State from whose facility the launch takes place

Apply for the operational licence in the territorial launching State: France under Loi 2008-518, the UK under the Space Industry Act 2018, Italy under Law 89/2025, the Netherlands under the Wra, Belgium under the 2005 Act, or Luxembourg under the 2017 Act for space resources missions

Negotiate a bilateral responsibility-allocation or hold-harmless agreement between the operator's home State and the launching State so liability flows are predictable, especially where the home State lacks a comprehensive act (the German pattern)

Build technical compliance to ECSS standards (M, Q, E, U, S, I branches), which ESA contracts cascade into industry through the Procurement Regulations and which national regulators reference for debris mitigation, safety and product assurance
paragraphs2: After the EU Space Act applies in 2030, the sequence changes. A single authorisation in the Member State of establishment under Articles 6 to 10, plus a launch certification in the Member State of launch, will be mutually recognised across the Union for safety, resilience and sustainability. Constellations get a single authorisation under Article 49. Third country operators register in the Union Register of Space Objects, appoint an EU legal representative and apply for an e-certificate, or rely on Commission equivalence with their home jurisdiction. The treaty layer does not go away. Operators will still need to map the launching States under OST Article VII for liability, and Member States will continue to conclude bilateral responsibility-allocation agreements on matters outside the Regulation's scope.
